title: The Brawl
desc: A street fight over territory. You can break it up or join in.

steps.text:
Two groups of UnderWorlders are squared off in a narrow street, shouting about a boundary line that apparently shifted overnight. One side claims the alley is theirs. The other side claims the alley was always theirs and the first side knows it.

Both sides have approximately the same number of very large creatures with very short tempers.

A patrol guard is watching from a safe distance, eating something fried on a stick. "Boundary disputes. Happens every month. They'll either sort it out with words or fists. Either way, it's resolved by sundown."

Someone throws a rock.

options.label: Step between the groups — try to mediate
Join the side that seems right

steps.text:
You put yourself in the gap between the groups, hands up. This is either very brave or very stupid. Both sides stare at you.

"Who are you?" "A neutral party." "We don't have neutral parties in the UnderWorld."

But they pause. The rock-throwing stops. You ask the simplest question: who was here first? Both sides answer simultaneously. Neither is convincing.

"Then split it," you say. "Use the drainage grate as the line. It's a physical marker. Neither side moved it."

Both groups look at the drainage grate. It runs down the center of the alley. It's ugly, practical, and undeniably neutral.

Grumbling. Muttering. One by one, they accept. The grate becomes the boundary. Nobody's happy, which means it's fair.

The patrol guard finishes his fried stick. "That's the fastest resolution I've seen in three years. Usually takes at least one broken nose."

options.label: Leave before someone changes their mind

steps.text:
The left side has a better case — they've got a stone marker at the alley entrance with a date carved on it. The right side has volume. You slide in beside the marker group.

"Got your evidence right there," you say, pointing at the stone marker. The right side counters: "That marker was moved!" The left side counters: "It's a stone. It weighs four hundred pounds. WHO moved it?"

Argument. More shouting. Then someone on the right swings. You duck. The creature behind you doesn't. The brawl is on.

It's brief. Chaotic. Mostly pushing and grabbing rather than real fighting. Nobody wants to do serious damage — this is territorial, not personal. You take an elbow to the ribs and give one back.

When it's over, both sides are bruised, breathing hard, and oddly friendly. The right side concedes the marker's point. The boundary stays where it was.

"Same time next month," someone jokes. Nobody laughs. They all know it's true.

options.label: Nurse your ribs and head home
